source_paper_title: A Stochastic Sequential Quadratic Optimization Algorithm for Nonlinear-Equality-Constrained Optimization with Rank-Deficient Jacobians
source_paper_bibtex: @article{Berahas_2024, title={A Stochastic Sequential Quadratic Optimization Algorithm for Nonlinear-Equality-Constrained Optimization with Rank-Deficient Jacobians}, volume={49}, ISSN={1526-5471}, url={http://dx.doi.org/10.1287/moor.2021.0154}, DOI={10.1287/moor.2021.0154}, number={4}, journal={Mathematics of Operations Research}, publisher={Institute for Operations Research and the Management Sciences (INFORMS)}, author={Berahas, Albert S. and Curtis, Frank E. and O’Neill, Michael J. and Robinson, Daniel P.}, year={2024}, month=nov, pages={2212–2248} }
context:
Section 4.4 (Complementary Events), page 25.

However, it remains an open question whether it is possible to obtain a similar guarantee if/when a subsequence of stochastic gradient estimates diverges.
title: Convergence guarantees when stochastic gradient estimates diverge in vanishing-merit regime
background_latex:
Consider the stochastic equality-constrained optimization problem
\[
\min_{x\in \mathbb{R}^n} f(x) \quad \text{s.t.}\quad c(x)=0,
\]
where \(f(x)=\mathbb{E}[F(x,\iota)]\) for a random variable \(\iota\) and smooth functions \(f:\mathbb{R}^n\to\mathbb{R}\), \(c:\mathbb{R}^n\to\mathbb{R}^m\). Let \(J(x):=\nabla c(x)^T\in\mathbb{R}^{m\times n}\) be the constraint Jacobian.

Define the constraint-violation measure
\[
\varphi(x):=\|c(x)\|_2.
\]
(Equivalently, one may consider \(\|c(x)\|_2^2\); stationarity conditions scale accordingly.) A point \(x\) is stationary for \(\varphi\) if either \(c(x)=0\) or \(c(x)\neq 0\) and
\[
\nabla \varphi(x)= J(x)^T\frac{c(x)}{\|c(x)\|_2}=0.
\]

An iterative stochastic SQP-type method generates iterates \(\{x_k\}_{k\ge 0}\) and uses stochastic gradient estimates \(g_k\) for \(\nabla f(x_k)\), e.g., unbiased with finite second moment,
\[
\mathbb{E}_k[g_k]=\nabla f(x_k),\qquad \mathbb{E}_k[\|g_k-\nabla f(x_k)\|_2^2]\le M,
\]
where \(\mathbb{E}_k[\cdot]\) is conditional expectation given the algorithm has reached \(x_k\).

The method employs an exact-penalty merit function
\[
\phi(x,\tau)= \tau f(x)+\|c(x)\|_2,
\]
with an adaptively updated merit parameter \(\tau_k\ge 0\). One regime of interest is when \(\tau_k\to 0\) (the algorithm places vanishing weight on the objective term). In analyses where \(\tau_k\to 0\), a key additional assumption that enables convergence is that the stochastic gradient errors remain uniformly bounded along the run, i.e., \(\sup_k \|g_k-\nabla f(x_k)\|_2<\infty\).

Define the problematic event
\[
E_{\tau,\mathrm{zero,bad}}:=\{\tau_k\to 0\ \text{and}\ \sup_{k\ge 0}\|g_k-\nabla f(x_k)\|_2=\infty\},
\]
i.e., the merit parameter vanishes while a subsequence of stochastic gradient estimates diverges away from the true gradients.
problem_definition_latex:
Develop convergence guarantees for stochastic SQP-type methods for equality-constrained problems in the event
\[
E_{\tau,\mathrm{zero,bad}}:=\{\tau_k\to 0\ \text{and}\ \sup_{k\ge 0}\|g_k-\nabla f(x_k)\|_2=\infty\}.
\]
In particular, establish conditions under which one can still guarantee that the iterates satisfy an asymptotic stationarity property for the constraint-violation measure \(\varphi(x)=\|c(x)\|_2\) (e.g., \(\liminf_{k\to\infty}\|J(x_k)^T c(x_k)\|_2=0\), or another appropriate stationarity measure), despite the divergence of the stochastic gradient estimation errors while \(\tau_k\to 0\).

open_problem_quote: However, it remains an open question whether it is possible to obtain a similar guarantee if/when a subsequence of stochastic gradient estimates diverges.
counterexample_suitability_rationale: The question asks for a positive convergence guarantee under an ill-behaved noise regime, not a sharp universal inequality that can be refuted by a finite-dimensional instance search. While one could search for pathological examples, the space of algorithms/noise processes is too unconstrained for an automated finite counterexample hunt to be decisive.
alphaevolve_suitability_rationale: One can programmatically explore toy problem families, noise models that induce divergent gradient estimates, and modified update rules for \(\tau_k\) or stepsizes, with a computable fitness signal based on observed constraint-stationarity measures. However, translating empirical behavior into general guarantees is indirect and would likely require additional human theoretical insight.

verification.notice: Generated from the paper text only and not independently verified as still open.

literature_review.synthesis:
In Berahas--Curtis--O’Neill--Robinson’s stochastic SQP framework for equality constraints with potentially rank-deficient Jacobians, the exact-penalty merit \(\phi(x,\tau)=\tau f(x)+\|c(x)\|_2\) is paired with a normal/tangential step decomposition. Their convergence theory splits into regimes depending on the merit parameter sequence \(\{\tau_k\}\): when \(\tau_k\) stabilizes at a small positive value, one can obtain expected stationarity for the original constrained problem; when \(\tau_k\to 0\), the algorithm is meant to transition to minimizing the feasibility measure \(\varphi(x)=\|c(x)\|_2\), and they prove \(\liminf_k\|J(x_k)^T c(x_k)\|=0\) under an additional assumption that stochastic gradient errors remain bounded. The open gap is precisely the adverse event \(E_{\tau,\mathrm{zero,bad}}\) where \(\tau_k\to 0\) while \(\sup_k\|g_k-\nabla f(x_k)\|=\infty\), which disrupts the intended decoupling between feasibility progress (normal step) and objective information (tangential step).

Forward work closest to the vanishing-merit setting is the “Optimistic noise-aware sequential quadratic programming…” paper, which proves liminf infeasible-stationarity bounds for \(\|J(x_k)^T c(x_k)\|\) in the \(\tau_k\to 0\) regime even with rank-deficient Jacobians, but only under uniformly bounded noise assumptions, so it still excludes \(E_{\tau,\mathrm{zero,bad}}\). Several stochastic SQP/trust-region lines of work (e.g., “Fully stochastic trust-region SQP…”, “High probability complexity bounds… with heavy-tailed noise”, and “A Trust-Region Interior-Point Stochastic SQP Method”) relax almost-sure boundedness by using probabilistic accuracy events tied to a shrinking trust-region radius; these frameworks can accommodate realized runs with \(\sup_k\|g_k-\nabla f(x_k)\|=\infty\) while still proving almost-sure or high-probability KKT-type stationarity, but they do not analyze the exact-penalty vanishing-\(\tau_k\) mechanism nor directly target stationarity of \(\varphi(x)=\|c(x)\|\) conditioned on the bad event.

Overall, the problem remains open: existing analyses either (i) rule out \(\tau_k\to 0\) by proving merit-parameter stabilization under bounded errors, (ii) analyze \(\tau_k\to 0\) but require uniform boundedness of gradient/Jacobian noise, or (iii) allow heavy-tailed/unbounded errors via probabilistic accuracy conditions but do not couple this with a vanishing-objective-weight exact-penalty regime. A promising direction is to blend the Berahas et al. normal/tangential decomposition and feasibility-stationarity target \(\liminf\|J^T c\|=0\) with probabilistic-oracle or robust-estimation machinery (e.g., truncation, median-of-means, or accuracy-forcing) that ensures sufficiently many iterations have controlled tangential noise even if \(\sup_k\|g_k-\nabla f(x_k)\|=\infty\), thereby recovering a supermartingale-type descent for \(\|c(x)\|\) on a subsequence without assuming uniform boundedness.

sources.title: Optimistic noise-aware sequential quadratic programming for equality constrained optimization with rank-deficient jacobians
sources.theorem_or_result:
The paper analyzes an SQP method for equality-constrained optimization with bounded noise in objective/constraints and possibly rank-deficient Jacobians, using an exact \(\ell_2\) merit function \(\bar\phi(x,\bar\tau)=\bar\tau\,\bar f(x)+\|\bar c(x)\|\) with nonincreasing \(\bar\tau_k\). In the regime \(\bar\tau_k\to 0\), it proves (Theorem 3.38) that the iterates satisfy a liminf infeasible-stationarity guarantee for the true constraint-violation measure \(\phi(x)=\tfrac12\|c(x)\|^2\):
\[
\liminf_{k\to\infty}\|J(x_k)^T c(x_k)\| \le \max\Big\{\epsilon_c(\epsilon_J+\kappa_J)+\kappa_c\epsilon_J + \sqrt{\tfrac{(1+\sigma_\gamma)(\kappa_c+\epsilon_c)E_c^i}{\eta\,\alpha_{l0}^i\,\sigma_c\sigma_v}},\; 2\kappa_J\epsilon_c\Big\},
\]
where \(E_c^i\) depends on the constraint/Jacobian noise and step-size option. In the complementary regime \(\bar\tau_k\ge \bar\tau_{\min}>0\), it proves averaged bounds (Theorem 3.36) implying \(\frac1{k+1}\sum_{j=0}^k\|J(x_j)^T c(x_j)\|^2\) is \(O(1/(k+1))+O(\epsilon_f+\epsilon_g+\epsilon_c+\epsilon_J)\).
sources.connection: The open problem asks for convergence guarantees for stochastic SQP when the merit parameter vanishes (\(\tau_k\to 0\)) while stochastic gradient errors can be unbounded (event \(\sup_k\|g_k-\nabla f(x_k)\|=\infty\)). This paper directly studies the \(\tau_k\to 0\) (rank-deficient) regime and proves that, even then, one can guarantee asymptotic (liminf) stationarity for the constraint-violation objective \(\|c(x)\|\) (equivalently \(\tfrac12\|c(x)\|^2\)) via bounds on \(\|J(x_k)^T c(x_k)\|\) (Theorem 3.38). However, its noise model assumes uniformly bounded errors in gradients/Jacobians (Assumption 2.2: \(\|\bar g(x)-\nabla f(x)\|\le \epsilon_g\) for all iterates), which excludes the open-problem event where gradient errors diverge. Thus it provides partial progress: it establishes the desired type of infeasible-stationarity guarantee in the vanishing-merit regime, but not under unbounded stochastic-gradient deviations.

sources.title: Inexact sequential quadratic optimization for minimizing a stochastic objective function subject to deterministic nonlinear equality constraints
sources.theorem_or_result:
They propose SISQO (stochastic inexact SQO) for 
\(\min f(x)=\mathbb E[F(x,\omega)]\) s.t. \(c(x)=0\) with deterministic smooth constraints and stochastic objective gradients \(g_k\). Under Assumptions 1–6 and on the event \(E_{\tau,\mathrm{low}}\) that the merit parameter becomes constant at a sufficiently small positive value \(\tau_k\equiv \tau_{k_{\tau,\xi}}>0\) (and \(\xi_k\) stabilizes), Theorem 1 proves an expected stationarity guarantee in terms of the “true-step” model reduction:
- If \(\beta_k\equiv\beta\in(0, A/((1-\eta)(A+\theta)))\), then
\[\mathbb E_{\tau,\mathrm{low}}\Big[\tfrac1K\sum_{j=k_{\tau,\xi}+1}^{k_{\tau,\xi}+K} \Delta l(x_j,\tau_{k_{\tau,\xi}},\nabla f_j,d^{\mathrm{true}}_j)\Big]\le \frac{\beta M}{A-(1-\eta)(A+\theta)\beta}+o(1).\]
- If \(\sum \beta_k=\infty\) and \(\sum \beta_k^2<\infty\), then
\[\lim_{K\to\infty}\mathbb E_{\tau,\mathrm{low}}\Big[\frac{\sum_{j} \beta_j\,\Delta l(x_j,\tau_{k_{\tau,\xi}},\nabla f_j,d^{\mathrm{true}}_j)}{\sum_j \beta_j}\Big]=0.\]
Moreover \(\Delta l(x_k,\tau,\nabla f_k,d^{\mathrm{true}}_k)\ge \kappa_l\tau(\|u^{\mathrm{true}}_k\|^2+\|c_k\|)\), so vanishing averages imply feasibility and first-order KKT stationarity along subsequences.
sources.connection: The open problem asks for convergence/stationarity guarantees specifically on the adverse event \(\{\tau_k\to 0\ \text{and}\ \sup_k\|g_k-\nabla f(x_k)\|=\infty\}\), i.e., a vanishing-merit regime with potentially unbounded stochastic-gradient errors. This paper explicitly does not analyze that regime: in Section 3.3 it states there are three possibilities for \(\{\tau_k\}\) (eventually constant small, vanishing, or constant too large) and then restricts the convergence theory to the first case (Event \(E_{\tau,\mathrm{low}}\)), where \(\tau_k\) becomes constant at a sufficiently small positive value. It cites prior discussion ([3]) that \(\tau_k\to 0\) can be ruled out if gradient errors are uniformly bounded, but it provides no guarantees when errors are unbounded and \(\tau_k\to 0\). Thus it does not solve the open problem; it provides related convergence analysis conditional on avoiding the vanishing-\(\tau\) regime, and clarifies that the problematic event remains untreated.

sources.title: High probability complexity bounds of trust-region stochastic sequential quadratic programming with heavy-tailed noise
sources.theorem_or_result:
The paper proposes a trust-region stochastic SQP (TR-SSQP) method for 
\(\min_x f(x)\) s.t. \(c(x)=0\) with stochastic (possibly biased) and heavy-tailed oracle noise, and proves high-probability iteration complexity bounds: it finds a first-order \(\varepsilon\)-stationary point (in KKT residual) in \(O(\varepsilon^{-2})\) iterations and a second-order \(\varepsilon\)-stationary point in \(O(\varepsilon^{-3})\) iterations, under probabilistic oracle conditions allowing irreducible noise and heavy-tailed objective-value noise with bounded \((1+\delta)\)-moment (Theorem 4.17, Corollaries 4.18–4.19).
sources.connection: The open problem asks for convergence guarantees in the regime where an exact-penalty merit parameter \(\tau_k\to 0\) while stochastic gradient errors \(\|g_k-\nabla f(x_k)\|\) are unbounded (event \(E_{\tau,\mathrm{zero,bad}}\)). This paper is related in that it studies stochastic SQP with merit/trust-region acceptance under heavy-tailed noise and does not require bounded gradient errors in the usual almost-sure sense; instead it assumes a per-iteration high-probability accuracy event for gradient estimates: \(\|\bar g_k-g_k\|\le g+\kappa_g\Delta_k^{\alpha+1}\) with probability \(\ge 1-p_g\) (Definition 3.2). This rules out the specific ‘diverging gradient estimates’ event along the realized run, because the analysis conditions on having sufficiently many accurate iterations and controls the number of inaccurate ones probabilistically. Moreover, the algorithm/analysis is trust-region based and targets KKT stationarity for the constrained problem, not stationarity of the pure feasibility measure \(\varphi(x)=\|c(x)\|\) in a vanishing-merit (\(\tau_k\to 0\)) penalty regime. Hence it does not directly provide guarantees on \(\liminf\|J(x_k)^T c(x_k)\|\) under \(E_{\tau,\mathrm{zero,bad}}\), but it offers techniques (martingale inequalities for heavy-tailed noise; handling irreducible bias) that could be relevant for developing such guarantees.

sources.title: A Trust-Region Interior-Point Stochastic Sequential Quadratic Programming Method
sources.theorem_or_result:
The paper proposes TR-IP-SSQP, a trust-region interior-point stochastic SQP method for 
\(\min_x \mathbb{E}[F(x;\xi)]\) subject to deterministic nonlinear equalities \(c(x)=0\) and inequalities \(h(x)\le 0\). Under Assumptions 4.1 (smoothness, bounded iterates, full row rank of the combined Jacobian matrix \(A_k\)) and 4.2 (eventual stabilization of the merit parameter \(\bar\mu_k\)), and probabilistic oracle conditions (Definitions 3.1–3.2) allowing heavy-tailed noise, Theorem 4.11 proves almost-sure first-order stationarity for the barrier subproblems:
- \(\liminf_{k\to\infty}\|\nabla L_{\theta_k}(x_k,s_k,\lambda_k,\tau_k)\|=0\) a.s., where \(\theta_k\downarrow 0\) is a prescribed barrier sequence.
Moreover, any limit point \(x^*\) of a subsequence with \(\|\nabla L_{\theta_{k_\ell}}\|\to 0\) that satisfies LICQ for the original problem is a KKT point of the original constrained problem.
sources.connection:
The open problem asks for convergence guarantees in the regime \(	au_k	o 0\) (vanishing weight on the objective in an exact-penalty merit \(\phi(x,	au)=	au f(x)+\|c(x)\|\)) even when stochastic gradient errors are unbounded along the run (event \(\sup_k\|g_k-
abla f(x_k)\|=\infty\)).

This paper is relevant because it explicitly targets stochastic constrained optimization with adaptive sampling and allows gradient estimators with unbounded variance (Remark 3.3). Its probabilistic first-order oracle only requires \(\| ar g_k-
abla f(x_k)\|\le \kappa_g\Delta_k\) with fixed probability \(\ge 1-p_g\), not uniform boundedness almost surely. Since Corollary 4.10 shows \(\Delta_k	o 0\) a.s., the oracle implies that along infinitely many iterations the gradient error is small, while it may be arbitrarily large on the complement events—so \(\sup_k\| ar g_k-
abla f(x_k)\|\) can be infinite without violating assumptions.

However, the paper does not analyze the exact-penalty merit parameter \(	au_k\) going to zero; instead it uses an \(\ell_2\)-penalty parameter \( ar\mu_k\) (assumed stabilized) and an interior-point barrier parameter \(	heta_k\downarrow 0\). The stationarity guarantee is for the barrier KKT residual \(\|
abla L_{	heta_k}\|\), not directly for constraint-violation stationarity \(
abla\|c(x)\|\) under \(	au_k	o 0\). Thus it provides partial progress/techniques toward handling unbounded gradient errors, but does not resolve the specific vanishing-merit-parameter regime posed in the open problem.

sources.title: Fully stochastic trust-region sequential quadratic programming for equality-constrained optimization problems
sources.theorem_or_result:
They propose TR-StoSQP, a fully stochastic trust-region SQP method for 
\(\min f(x)=\mathbb E[F(x;\xi)]\) s.t. \(c(x)=0\), and prove global convergence of KKT residuals under standard smoothness/boundedness assumptions and a stochastic gradient growth condition. In particular, if the radius-related sequence \(\{ eta_k\}\subset(0, eta_{\max}]\) satisfies \(\sum_k  eta_k=\infty\) and \(\sum_k  eta_k^2<\infty\), then \(\|
abla L(x_k,\lambda_k)\|	o 0\) almost surely (Theorem 4.11), where \(
abla L=(
abla f(x)+G(x)^T\lambda,\ c(x))\) and \(\lambda_k\) is a least-squares multiplier estimate. For constant \( eta_k\equiv eta\), they show the expected averaged KKT residual converges to an \(O( eta)\) neighborhood (Theorem 4.9).
sources.connection: The open problem asks for convergence guarantees in the regime where an exact-penalty merit parameter \(\tau_k\to 0\) while stochastic gradient errors are unbounded (event \(E_{\tau,\mathrm{zero,bad}}\)). This paper is closely related in that it studies fully stochastic SQP with an \(\ell_2\) exact-penalty merit function and explicitly discusses merit-parameter stability. However, their global convergence analysis assumes the merit parameter stabilizes (Assumption 4.2), and they later prove this stability only under a bounded gradient-noise condition \(\|\bar g_k-\nabla f(x_k)\|\le M_1\) (Assumption 4.12). Thus, they do not cover the problematic event where gradient errors diverge while the merit weight on the objective vanishes. Still, their analysis techniques (trust-region control, decomposition into normal/tangential steps, and handling of stochastic error terms in merit decrease) provide partial progress/tools toward analyzing such regimes, but do not establish stationarity of the constraint-violation measure \(\varphi(x)=\|c(x)\|\) under unbounded gradient errors.

sources.title: A sequential quadratic programming method for optimization with stochastic objective functions, deterministic inequality constraints and robust subproblems
sources.theorem_or_result:
The paper proposes a stochastic SQP method (Algorithm 2) for 
\[\min_x f(x)=\mathbb{E}[F(x,\xi)]\ \text{s.t. } h(x)=0,\ c(x)\le 0\]
using a stochastic line search and an exact-penalty merit function \(\Psi(x;\rho)=f(x)+\rho\,\mathrm{dist}((h(x),c(x))\mid K)\). Under Assumptions 2.2 (smoothness/bounded iterates), 3.3 (probabilistically accurate gradient/function estimates), and 3.13 (extended MFCQ at accumulation points), it proves almost sure asymptotic stationarity:
\[\liminf_{k\to\infty}\big(\varphi(\hat x_k)^2+\chi(\hat x_k)^2\big)=0\ \text{a.s.}\]
(Corollary 3.28), where \(\varphi\) measures constraint violation and \(\chi\) measures first-order optimality for the constrained problem. It also proves \(\Pr(\lim_{k\to\infty}\hat\rho_k=+\infty)=0\) (Theorem 3.30) via a sampling/accuracy-forcing scheme.
sources.connection:
The open problem asks for convergence guarantees in the regime where the merit parameter on the objective vanishes (\(\tau_k\to 0\)) while stochastic gradient errors can become unbounded (\(\sup_k\|g_k-\nabla f(x_k)\|=\infty\)), and still to guarantee asymptotic stationarity for the constraint-violation measure \(\|c(x)\|\).

This paper is closely related but addresses a different penalty-parameter pathology: it uses an exact penalty \(f+\rho\varphi\) with \(\rho_k\) potentially \emph{blowing up} due to noisy gradients, and designs a sampling/accuracy-forcing mechanism to ensure \(\rho_k\not\to\infty\) almost surely (Theorem 3.30). Its convergence results (Corollary 3.28) are proved under a probabilistic accuracy condition of the form \(\|g_k-\nabla f(x_k)\|\le \varepsilon_g\alpha_k\|d_k\|\) with high conditional probability, which effectively prevents arbitrarily bad gradients from occurring too often.

Thus, it does not analyze the event \(\tau_k\to 0\) with unbounded gradient errors; instead it enforces sampling so that the bad-gradient events are summable (Borel–Cantelli) and rules out the analogous “bad” event for its own penalty parameter. The techniques (accuracy tied to step size, Borel–Cantelli control of rare large errors, and stationarity in terms of constraint violation \(\varphi\)) are potentially useful tools toward the open problem, but the paper does not provide guarantees \emph{conditioned on} the bad event occurring, nor does it treat a vanishing objective-weight parameter.
